system_prompt: You are an analyst. Read the brief carefully and provide your best-reasoned recommendation.
user_prompt:
# Task C - Health app data privacy policy

## Brief

A direct-to-consumer health app (~2M monthly active users) is launching an AI-powered health coaching feature. The product team must choose a primary data policy that governs how user health data is processed by the AI. The choice has implications for capability, privacy, regulatory exposure, cost, and trust. The CEO asks for a primary recommendation.

## Options (elements E1-E5, anonymized later)

**Option A:** Local-only processing. All AI inference happens on the user's device. No health data leaves the phone. The model is smaller (capability ceiling 60-70% of cloud-equivalent) but privacy is structurally maximal.

**Option B:** Encrypted cloud with no training. User health data sent to the cloud encrypted, decrypted only at inference time in a confidential-computing enclave, never used to train any model. Full capability, ~3x infrastructure cost.

**Option C:** Cloud with opt-out training. Data sent to the cloud, used for personalization by default, used for model improvement unless user opts out. Full capability, standard cost, regulatory grey area in some jurisdictions.

**Option D:** Cloud with opt-in training only. Data sent to the cloud and used for personalization; used for model improvement only with explicit opt-in. Default-on opt-in flow with clear UX. Capability slightly degraded vs C by default (less training data).

**Option E:** Federated learning. Personalization happens locally; aggregate model updates sent to cloud, individual data does not leave the device. Privacy preserved at architectural level. Capability and reliability still under active research; deployment complexity is high.

## Task for each agent

Recommend the most appropriate policy and explain why. 200-400 words. Advocate a position rather than enumerating all options.

## Why this task

- Different domain from A (capital allocation) and B (organizational diagnosis): product ethics with regulatory and technical dimensions.
- Multi-axis tradeoffs: privacy / capability / cost / trust / complexity.
- No clear right answer; reasonable agents will weight axes differently.
- 5 elements suitable for triadic procedure.
- Cross-validates whether method works on a third domain not seen in pilot.


Provide your recommendation in 200-400 words. Advocate for a specific position; do not enumerate all options.
raw_response_content:
# Recommendation: Option B — Encrypted Cloud with Confidential Computing, No Training

For a direct-to-consumer health app at 2M MAU launching AI coaching, **Option B** is the right primary policy. Here's the case.

Health data is the most sensitive category most users will ever share with an app. Trust is the binding constraint on this product's growth, not capability or cost. A single breach or regulatory action (HIPAA in the US, GDPR Article 9 in the EU, emerging AI-specific rules) doesn't just incur fines — it destroys the category permission this app depends on. Option B is the only choice that delivers full coaching capability while making a clean, defensible promise: *your health data is never used to train anything, and is only ever decrypted inside an attested enclave.* That sentence fits on a marketing page and survives legal scrutiny in every major jurisdiction.

Why not the alternatives? **Option A** sacrifices 30-40% of capability — for a health coach, that gap shows up as worse advice, which has real welfare costs and weakens product-market fit. **Option C** is a trust time-bomb: opt-out training on health data is already legally precarious in the EU and increasingly indefensible reputationally; one journalist's exposé ends the product. **Option D** is the conventional "responsible" choice and a reasonable second pick, but it still requires defending why health data should ever train a shared model, and it complicates the privacy story ("we train on some users' data") right when clarity matters most. **Option E** is research-grade; federated learning's privacy guarantees in practice (against gradient inversion, membership inference) are still contested, and deployment at 2M MAU with the complexity overhead is not where a product team should be spending its innovation budget on a launch.

The ~3x infrastructure cost is real but bounded and scales with revenue. Confidential computing (AWS Nitro, Azure Confidential VMs, GCP Confidential Space) is now mature enough for production. The capability ceiling matches competitors, the regulatory posture is conservative across jurisdictions, and the trust narrative is the strongest available.

Later, once trust is established, the team can offer **opt-in** training as a separate, well-consented program for users who want to contribute. But the *default* policy at launch should be Option B.
